title: Dump uses recovered panics as its only termination condition
reasoning: Dump has no explicit length or maximum index and stops only when safeCalculate recovers any panic. That creates two concrete failure modes in tests: an Indicator implementation that never panics will hang the test process indefinitely, while an in-range panic from a real calculation bug is swallowed and converted into a shorter result slice. IndicatorEquals then only compares that truncated slice, so a test with a matching short expected slice can pass even though Calculate panicked.
reproduction: Create an Indicator whose Calculate always returns decimal.ONE and call Dump; the loop never terminates. Create one that returns a value at index 0 and panics at index 1, then call IndicatorEquals with expected []float64{1}; the panic is hidden by safeCalculate.
recommendation: Do not use a blanket recover as the sequence boundary. Make Dump accept an explicit count, or make IndicatorEquals iterate exactly len(expected) values and let Calculate panics fail the test. If out-of-range probing is still needed, recover only in a narrowly scoped helper with a documented sentinel behavior that cannot hide in-range calculation failures.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The feature payload lists no linked tests for pkg/testutils, and the current helper behavior itself converts panics into normal termination rather than an assertion failure.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a test-only Indicator that panics at index 1 after returning one value, and assert that the fixed IndicatorEquals/Dump path reports the panic or fails instead of silently returning a one-element slice. Add a bounded/non-panicking Indicator case to prove Dump no longer hangs.
minimumFixScope: Change pkg/testutils Dump/IndicatorEquals/safeCalculate termination semantics and update any tests that relied on panic-at-end iteration to pass an explicit expected length or count.
